mutation.note: mutmut refuses to run on native Windows (`mutmut --version` prints 'To run mutmut on Windows, please use the WSL' -- verified this session), matching the project's own CI comment that mutation testing is deliberately deferred to the Linux CI job (pipeline-ci.yml's `mutation` job, currently `if: false` pending <MUTATION_CMD>/<MUTATION_SCOPE>). Not run locally this session; scope could not be exercised at all (0 of configured_scope), so quality_ok is honestly false rather than claimed true against an unmeasured scope.

gaps.gap: RESOLVED this session: debugging root-caused the true bottleneck (chunk-per-row streaming fanning out through 4 nested BaseHTTPMiddleware layers, not CSV encoding) and fixed it within the approved design (batched chunking, precompiled str.translate escape, tuned yield_per). The HUMAN confirmed a revised, binding budget of p95 <= 3,000ms at the escalation (superseding the original 500ms aspiration, which the debug notes show is not achievable within the mandated constant-memory streaming design). The perf test now hard-asserts the confirmed bound over a 10-sample stable measurement (this session's re-run: p95 1,643.87ms, min 1,093.57ms, median 1,385.42ms -- comfortably under 3,000ms and consistent with debug-notes' own stable-sample figures). Remaining residual gap: only 10 samples in this run (smoke-sized, single concurrency=1 client) -- a wider/longer soak or concurrent-client load run would build more confidence in production but is not required by the confirmed AC wording.

The chunk-count>1 assertion is a reasonable proxy for 'not fully buffered' but does not directly measure peak memory. A future regression that buffers the whole result client-side but still happens to flush in more than one TCP segment (e.g. due to buffering N rows at a time rather than 1) would still pass this test. A memory-ceiling assertion (e.g. tracemalloc peak during the stream) would close this more tightly.

No test exercises a combination of ALL four filters simultaneously (customer_id + metric + from + to together) -- each filter is tested in isolation. The shared `_export_filter_clause_and_params` helper makes this low-risk (the WHERE-clause construction is compositional and unit-testable by inspection), but an integration test combining all four would remove the remaining inference.

No test drives a client disconnect mid-stream against the real integration server (running_server fixture) to observe the `completed=False` log path under real ASGI/ASGI-middleware conditions -- the unit test simulates this by raising inside a mocked generator, which proves the finally-block logic but not that Starlette's real disconnect handling reaches it the same way in production.

Coverage reports this exact line as missed despite count_usage_rollups being called by many passing tests (cap-boundary, cap-exceeded, filter tests) -- worth a follow-up look (likely a coverage/line-mapping artifact from the f-string SQL literal spanning multiple source lines, not a real untested branch, but not independently confirmed this session).
